attributes.description: The legal or commonly-used name of the account.
attributes.name: accountName
attributes.usage: Primary identifier shown across nearly every CRM view, list, search result, and report. Drives duplicate-detection rules, search relevance, and merge decisions. Required because virtually no business process can proceed without a recognizable label for the organization. In CiviCRM this is `organization_name` (not `name`) when contact_type is Organization. In Bloomerang the equivalent is `FullName` for Organization-type constituents.

attributes.description: Categorizes the relationship the organization has with the user's business.
attributes.name: accountType
attributes.notes: In CiviCRM this maps to contact_sub_type rather than a separate Type attribute. In Bloomerang this is the Type discriminator that selects Individual vs Organization.
attributes.usage: Drives segmentation, reporting, and conditional UI behavior — for example, hiding sales-pipeline fields on vendor accounts. Enum values vary across systems (Salesforce ships Customer-Direct, Customer-Channel, Channel Partner, Reseller, Installation Partner, Technology Partner, Other; EspoCRM ships Customer, Investor, Partner, Reseller; HubSpot uses lifecyclestage which conflates type and pipeline stage). Often the first attribute customized after deployment. In nonprofit systems this is reframed as Constituent Type (donor / volunteer / foundation / corporate sponsor / etc.).

attributes.name: industry
attributes.usage: Used for segmentation, pricing, sales territory assignment, regulatory compliance routing, and reporting. Long-tail vocabulary varies widely across systems — EspoCRM ships ~50 industry options including detailed sector breakdowns; Salesforce's standard list is ~40; HubSpot uses a slightly different vocabulary aligned to its persona templates. NTEE codes (National Taxonomy of Exempt Entities) often replace this for nonprofit accounts.

attributes.description: Primary public website URL for the account.
attributes.name: website
attributes.usage: Anchors many automation flows: domain-based duplicate detection (HubSpot and Attio both use the website domain to auto-match new contacts to existing companies), enrichment lookups (LinkedIn, Crunchbase, ZoomInfo keys), grant-eligibility verification for nonprofits (verifying a foundation's claimed website matches their public-facing identity).

attributes.name: phone
attributes.usage: Office or main line — distinct from individual contacts' direct phones. Used for routing inbound calls, reverse-lookup matching, and address-verification workflows. EspoCRM models this as a multi-type phoneNumber field with subtypes (Office, Mobile, Fax, Other).

attributes.description: Main organizational email address (e.g. info@, contact@).
attributes.name: emailAddress
attributes.usage: Generic-recipient address (info@, contact@, hello@) distinct from personal addresses on Contact records. Used for catch-all communication, list-import targeting, and email-deliverability validation. Some systems (HubSpot, Attio) don't provide a first-class email attribute on Company/Account at all — assuming all email goes to individual Contacts.

attributes.description: Composite address (street, city, state, country, postal code) used for billing and invoicing.
attributes.name: billingAddress
attributes.notes: Address modeling varies dramatically. CiviCRM and Bloomerang model Address as a separate first-class entity allowing multiple addresses per record (Home, Billing, Mailing, etc.). Salesforce and EspoCRM use composite fields with billing/shipping doubles. HubSpot uses flat property fields.
attributes.usage: Composite of street, city, state, postal code, country. Salesforce and EspoCRM treat this as a first-class composite field with sub-fields automatically generated. HubSpot and Attio model address as separate individual properties (street, city, state, etc.). Drives invoice generation, tax jurisdiction calculation, and shipping-versus-billing address differentiation.

attributes.description: Composite address used for shipping or physical mail delivery.
attributes.name: shippingAddress
attributes.usage: Distinct from billing address when an account ships goods to a different location than where invoices are sent. Less commonly populated; many deployments collapse this into billing address. In nonprofit deployments this often becomes "mailing address" for direct-mail solicitations.

attributes.description: Free-form long-text description of the account.
attributes.name: description
attributes.usage: Catch-all narrative field for context that doesn't fit any structured attribute — relationship history, internal notes, account strategy, mission summary. Not typically used in automation or reporting; primarily a human-readable scratchpad. In Attio this attribute is enriched automatically with a brief AI-generated summary.

attributes.name: numberOfEmployees
attributes.usage: Used for company-size segmentation, sales-territory assignment, and pricing tiers. In Attio represented as an "Employee range" enriched attribute (1-10, 11-50, etc.) rather than a numeric value. Most useful for B2B sales motions; rarely populated for nonprofit donor management.

attributes.description: Estimated annual revenue of the organization.
attributes.name: annualRevenue
attributes.usage: Drives sales-territory assignment, account scoring, and pricing tier selection. In Attio modeled as "Estimated ARR" with a range value (enriched). Frequently used in B2B sales motions for ICP scoring; typically irrelevant for nonprofit donor records.

attributes.description: Self-referential lookup to a parent account in a corporate hierarchy.
attributes.name: parentAccount
attributes.usage: Models corporate hierarchies (subsidiary → parent), enabling rolled-up reporting (sum opportunities across all child accounts of a parent) and consolidated relationship views. Critical in enterprise B2B contexts with complex corporate structures. In Attio handled via custom relationship attributes rather than a built-in self-reference.

attributes.description: The user who owns or is responsible for the account.
attributes.name: assignedUser
attributes.usage: Drives sharing rules, sales-team assignment, escalation routing, and "my accounts" filtering. Almost universal across CRMs because every record needs an accountable user for permission and pipeline-management reasons. Salesforce calls this OwnerId; HubSpot calls it hubspot_owner_id; EspoCRM calls it assignedUserId. Bloomerang assigns ownership at the database level rather than per-record.

attributes.description: System-generated timestamp of when the record was created.
attributes.name: createdAt
attributes.usage: Audit and analytics field; almost always read-only and system-managed. Drives "new accounts this month" reports, vintage cohort analysis, and data-quality checks (records older than X without activity).

attributes.description: System-generated timestamp of when the record was last updated.
attributes.name: lastModifiedAt
attributes.usage: Audit field; used in change-tracking workflows, integration sync filters (only pull records modified since last sync), and stale-data detection (records not touched in X months).

business_context: Account records anchor most relationship and revenue data in a CRM. They represent the parties the organization does business with: customers, prospects, vendors, partners, donors-as-organizations, foundations, schools, government bodies, and households. Most other CRM activity (opportunities, donations, cases, contracts, campaign memberships) hangs off an Account either directly or through a related Contact, so the way Accounts are modeled shapes nearly every downstream report, automation, and integration. In nonprofit deployments the Account model often diverges significantly from for-profit deployments — household accounts treat individual donors as members of a family unit, organization accounts represent foundations or corporate sponsors, and some systems (CiviCRM, Bloomerang) collapse the Account/Contact distinction entirely. When interviewing clients, surface "Account" by asking which organizational units they need to track distinctly from people: companies, families, schools, vendors, partner agencies, granting bodies. If the answer is "we only track individuals," the deployment may not need a separate Account entity at all (and the target backend's choice matters more — CiviCRM and Bloomerang accommodate this naturally; Salesforce and HubSpot still require Accounts as anchors).
name: Account
purpose: An organizational unit the CRM tracks as a customer, prospect, supplier, partner, or — in nonprofit contexts — a household, foundation, or constituent organization. Together with Contact, one of two universal anchor entities in CRM data models.

relationships.description: An account has many contacts; each contact typically belongs to one account (with multi-account exceptions in some systems).
An account has many activities (calls, meetings, tasks, emails) logged against it.
An account has many notes attached to it.

sources.title: Attio: Manage standard objects (Companies system attributes)
EspoCRM: Account entity definition (source of truth)
CiviCRM: Contacts user guide (Organization, Household, Individual)
Salesforce NPSP: Organize Your Data Effectively Using NPSP Features
Bloomerang REST API documentation: Constituent endpoint

systems.name: Company
systems.notes: Built-in standard object, enabled by default. Auto-populates from email-domain matching when People records are synced. Many attributes are enriched automatically by Attio (description, categories, location, social media handles, employee range, ARR estimates).

systems.name: Constituent (Organization Type)
systems.notes: MAJOR DIVERGENCE. Bloomerang unifies individual donors and organization donors under a single Constituent entity with a Type discriminator (Individual vs Organization). There is no separate Account table. Organization-type constituents track FullName instead of FirstName/ LastName. Households are not a first-class concept — managed via Relationships between individual constituents.

systems.name: Organization (Contact subtype)
systems.notes: MAJOR DIVERGENCE. CiviCRM unifies Person, Household, and Organization under a single Contact entity with a contact_type discriminator. There is no separate Account table. Organization-only fields (organization_name, legal_name, sic_code) only apply when contact_type=Organization. Household is also a contact_type, distinct from Organization, used for family/group records.

systems.name: Account
systems.notes: Standard CRM-module entity. Includes built-in billingAddress and shippingAddress composite fields. Account.type enum ships with Customer / Investor / Partner / Reseller; can be extended.

systems.name: Company
systems.notes: HubSpot uses the term "Company" rather than "Account." Companies are typically auto-created from email domain matching when contacts are added. Standard CRM object alongside Contacts, Deals, and Tickets.

systems.name: Account
systems.notes: Foundational standard object. Supports record types for differentiating Customer, Prospect, Partner. Person Account is an optional alternate model that combines Account + Contact into a single record for B2C use cases — opt-in feature, not enabled by default.

systems.name: Account (Household, 1x1, or Organization Record Type)
systems.notes: NPSP adds three Account record types layered on Salesforce's standard Account object: Household Account (group of related individuals living together), 1-to-1 Account (auto-generated per individual contact), and Organization Account (foundations, corporate donors, schools). Default is Household Account when a contact is created without an account assignment. NPSP-specific rollup fields (lifetime giving, household giving) live on Household accounts.
